examples.query: What were the two main things the author worked on before college?
reference_contexts: Before college the two main things I worked on, outside of school, were writing and programming.

examples.query: What kind of machine did the author use for programming in 9th grade?
reference_contexts: The first programs I tried writing were on the IBM 1401 that our school district used for what was then called 'data processing.'

examples.query: What programming language was used on the IBM 1401?
reference_contexts: The language we used was an early version of Fortran.

examples.query: Why was the author puzzled by the 1401?
examples.reference_answer: Because the only form of input was data stored on punched cards, which he didn't have.
reference_contexts: I was puzzled by the 1401. I couldn't figure out what to do with it... The only form of input to programs was data stored on punched cards, and I didn't have any data stored on punched cards.

examples.query: What was the first microcomputer the author's friend built?
examples.reference_answer: A microcomputer built using a Heathkit.
reference_contexts: My friend Rich Draves built a microcomputer out of a Heathkit.

examples.query: What kind of computer did the author's father buy him?
reference_contexts: Then my dad bought me a TRS-80.

examples.query: What did the author write on his TRS-80?
examples.reference_answer: Simple games, a program to predict how high his model rockets would fly, and a word processor.
reference_contexts: I wrote simple games, a program to predict how high my model rockets would fly, and a word processor that my father used to write at least one book.

examples.query: What did the author originally plan to study in college?
reference_contexts: I started college planning to study philosophy.

examples.query: Why did the author switch from philosophy to AI?
examples.reference_answer: Because he thought AI was going to solve the mysteries of the mind.
reference_contexts: I switched to AI. I thought AI was going to solve the mind.

examples.query: Where did the author go to grad school?
reference_contexts: I applied to three grad schools: MIT, Harvard, and CMU... I went to Harvard.

examples.query: What book did the author write about Lisp?
reference_contexts: I wrote a book about Lisp hacking called On Lisp.

examples.query: What did the author realize about traditional AI during grad school?
reference_contexts: I realized that traditional AI was a hoax.

examples.query: What did the author decide to focus on instead of traditional AI?
reference_contexts: I decided to focus on Lisp.

examples.query: What art school did the author attend?
reference_contexts: I applied to RISD and was accepted.

examples.query: Where did the author go to study painting after attending RISD?
reference_contexts: I went to Florence to study painting.

examples.query: What software company did the author start with Robert Morris?
reference_contexts: Robert Morris and I started a company called Viaweb.

examples.query: What did Viaweb allow users to do?
examples.reference_answer: It allowed users to build online stores through a web browser.
reference_contexts: Viaweb allowed users to build online stores through a web browser.

examples.query: What programming language was Viaweb written in?
reference_contexts: Viaweb was written in Lisp.

examples.query: Which company acquired Viaweb?
reference_contexts: Yahoo bought Viaweb.

examples.query: What was Viaweb renamed to after the acquisition?
reference_contexts: They renamed it Yahoo Store.

examples.query: What did the author do immediately after leaving Yahoo?
examples.reference_answer: He went back to painting and writing essays.
reference_contexts: After leaving Yahoo I went back to painting and writing essays.

examples.query: What framework did the author build to write web apps in Lisp?
reference_contexts: I started working on a new dialect of Lisp called Arc.

examples.query: What investment firm did the author start?
reference_contexts: We started Y Combinator.

examples.query: Who were the co-founders of Y Combinator?
examples.reference_answer: Paul Graham, Jessica Livingston, Robert Morris, and Trevor Blackwell.
reference_contexts: The founders were me, Jessica Livingston, Robert Morris, and Trevor Blackwell.

examples.query: Where was the first Y Combinator batch located?
reference_contexts: The first batch was in Cambridge.

examples.query: What news aggregator did the author create?
reference_contexts: I wrote Hacker News.

examples.query: Why was Hacker News created?
examples.reference_answer: To see if Arc could handle a web app with many users.
reference_contexts: I wrote Hacker News to test Arc.

examples.query: What was Hacker News originally called?
reference_contexts: It was originally called Startup News.

examples.query: Where did Y Combinator eventually move to permanently?
reference_contexts: We moved YC to Silicon Valley permanently.

examples.query: What problem did Y Combinator solve for founders?
reference_contexts: We wanted to make it easy to get seed funding.

examples.query: Who took over Y Combinator when the author stepped down?
reference_contexts: I asked Sam Altman if he wanted to be president of YC.

examples.query: Why did the author decide to step down from Y Combinator?
examples.reference_answer: He was tired and his mother was sick.
reference_contexts: I was tired, and my mother was sick.

examples.query: What did the author do after retiring from YC?
examples.reference_answer: He started painting again and worked on a new dialect of Lisp.
reference_contexts: I started painting again, and working on Bel.

examples.query: What is the name of the new Lisp dialect the author created?
reference_contexts: I named the new Lisp dialect Bel.

examples.query: What was the author's main motivation for creating Bel?
examples.reference_answer: To write a formal definition of Lisp.
reference_contexts: I wanted to write a formal definition of Lisp.

examples.query: What did the author learn about painting in Florence?
examples.reference_answer: That painting is a rigorous discipline.
reference_contexts: I learned that painting is a rigorous discipline.

examples.query: What did the author learn from consulting at Interleaf?
examples.reference_answer: That he wanted to build his own company.
reference_contexts: I realized I wanted to build my own company.

examples.query: What was the author's attitude towards the dot-com bubble?
examples.reference_answer: He thought most of the internet companies were fake.
reference_contexts: I thought most of the internet companies were fake.

examples.query: What was the worst thing about leaving YC for the author?
reference_contexts: The worst thing about leaving YC was not working with Jessica anymore.

examples.query: What does the author think about the concept of invented vs discovered?
examples.reference_answer: He believes that fundamental concepts, like Lisp, are discovered rather than invented.
reference_contexts: I believe that Lisp is discovered, not invented.

examples.query: What did the author compare writing essays to?
reference_contexts: Writing essays is like debugging software.

examples.query: What was the first essay the author published online?
reference_contexts: I published The Python Paradox.

examples.query: Why did the author think his early short stories were deep?
examples.reference_answer: Because they had characters with strong feelings.
reference_contexts: My stories... had hardly any plot, just characters with strong feelings, which I imagined made them deep.

examples.query: Who did the author write Viaweb with?
reference_contexts: I wrote Viaweb with Robert Morris.
